subject: This Week in RWE, Causal Inference & AI — Date of April 22, 2026
body:
This Week in RWE, Causal Inference & AI

Five papers worth your time at the intersection of real-world evidence, causal inference, and artificial intelligence.

Romosozumab versus Teriparatide for Risk of Dementia in Individuals with Osteoporosis: A Target Trial Emulation Study

Hatano et al. — PubMed | Osteoporosis International, April 2026

Target trial emulation has arrived at one of the most provocative questions in geriatric pharmacoepidemiology: does the choice of anabolic osteoporosis therapy matter for dementia risk? Hatano et al. analyze 69,543 Japanese claims patients and find romosozumab initiation associated with a 24% lower relative risk of dementia than teriparatide in per-protocol analysis (RR 0.76), with Alzheimer's disease specifically at RR 0.77. The ITT/per-protocol divergence is the methodologically instructive finding: it signals that treatment duration mediates the effect, not merely assignment — which should change how we design future TTEs on chronic therapy questions.

Differences in Survival Associated With the First Antiseizure Medication in People With Dementia and Epilepsy

Zelano et al. — PubMed | Neurology, April 2026

This is the paper that should end routine valproate prescribing in patients with both dementia and epilepsy. Zelano et al. analyze 5,764 patients from Swedish national registers and find valproate associated with a 34% higher all-cause mortality hazard versus carbamazepine (aHR 1.34, 95% CI 1.20–1.48), while lamotrigine confers a 16% survival advantage (aHR 0.84). The three-method triangulation — Cox regression, propensity-score matching, and balancing weights all pointing the same direction — eliminates methodological artifact; the cardiovascular death mechanism (HR 1.30 for valproate vs. carbamazepine for CV death) is the clinically actionable insight.

Transforming Oncology Clinical Trial Matching Through Neuro-Symbolic, Multi-Agent AI and an Oncology-Specific Knowledge Graph

Loaiza-Bonilla et al. — PubMed | ESMO Real World Data and Digital Oncology, April 2026

Fewer than 5% of eligible adults enroll in oncology trials, and the bottleneck is largely the burden of manual eligibility screening. Loaiza-Bonilla et al. prospectively evaluate a neuro-symbolic multi-agent system — LLM extraction agents grounded in an oncology-specific knowledge graph — across 3,804 patients and 157,000+ clinical pages, achieving F1=0.82 versus GPT-4 zero-shot F1=0.47. The architectural lesson is the finding: pure LLMs hallucinate eligibility; knowledge graph grounding is what makes the system deployable at clinical scale, and the 7-point F1 subgroup gap between demographic groups should anchor every fairness discussion in AI-assisted trial enrollment.

Outcomes of Immune-Checkpoint Inhibitor Rechallenge in Metastatic Clear-Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma: Results From a Global Real-World Evidence Study

Cigliola et al. — PubMed | ESMO Open, April 2026

No prospective trial has randomized mccRCC patients on immune checkpoint inhibitor rechallenge timing, and none is coming. Cigliola et al. use the TriNetX federated database — 6,737 mccRCC patients, 288 with ≥2 ICI lines — and find rechallenge initiated ≥6 months after prior ICI associated with 3.6 months longer PFS and 15.5 months longer OS, confirmed after propensity matching (P=0.03). The timing signal is clinically actionable and methodologically clean: this is the right use case for federated real-world databases — sequencing questions that no single institution can power and no prospective trial will ever randomize.

Extent of Open Science Practices in the Reporting of Real World Evidence Research

Liu et al. — PubMed | International Journal of Population Data Science, April 2026

Zero out of forty-one. That is the number of RWE papers published over five years in a population data science journal that shared analytical code. Liu, Jorm, and colleagues audit open science practices in 41 IJPDS RWE articles from 2019–2024 and find no pre-registered protocols, no shared code, and only 34% with data availability statements. Code sharing is not a nicety in RWE — it is the minimum condition for regulatory agencies and HTA bodies to reproduce the analyses they are asked to trust, and this paper makes that deficit quantitative for the first time.
